macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Prior Discussion on Meitav and Negligence Liability

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context of Daf 10; background on meitav and property damage liability
The negligence principle (chavti bishmirato): when you are liable because you failed to watch and thus created the damage
Debate between Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish on liability for fire given to cheresh shoteh ve-katan

macro_segments.title: Comparative Analysis of Ox, Well, and Fire Liabilities—The Braita on Four Avot Nezikin

micro_segments.title: Introduction to braita comparing chomer (stringency) between shor and bor
Ways shor is weightier than bor: kofer payment, thirty shekel for slave, shor haniskal status
Characteristics making shor weightier: it goes out and damages, has propensity to gore, likely vs unlikely damage
Ways bor is weightier than shor: techilat asiyato lenezek (inherent hazard from inception), cheresh shoteh ve-katan liability
Comparison of shor to esh (fire): shor's monetary penalties vs fire's exemptions
Ways shor is weightier than fire: kofer, thirty shekel, shor haniskal, cheresh shoteh ve-katan liability
Ways fire is weightier than shor: muad mitechilato (inherent propensity from inception), eats fit and unfit things
Comparison of bor to esh: bor's inherent hazard vs fire's ability to spread and consume unfit items
Ways bor is weightier than fire: techilat asiyato lenezek, cheresh shoteh ve-katan liability
Ways fire is weightier than bor: darko leilech ulehazik, muad mitechilato, davar she'eino ra'ui lah (unfit items)

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Challenge on Omitted Comparisons: Keilim Liability and the Question of Rabbi Yehuda

micro_segments.title: Gemara challenges why keilim (vessels) were not listed as a difference: shor is chayav in keilim, bor is patur
Explanation that this braita reflects Rabbi Yehuda's view, who holds one is chayav in keilim by bor
Clarification of davar she'eino ra'ui lah: wood vs vessels—what fire normally consumes vs what it does not
Alternative: if not Rabbi Yehuda, then it is an incomplete list (Rabbanan view)
Additional omission: tamun (buried items) where ash is patur and shor is chayav
Tosafot's explanation: unlikely damage to fallow field and rocks; contrast to bor where field cannot fall in
Third omission from list: shor psulei hamukdashim (disqualified consecrated ox) liability by bor exemption

macro_segments.title: Hichsharti Bemiktzat Nizko: Partial Negligence and Full Liability—The Deepening Well Case

micro_segments.title: Mishna teaching: even if you prepared only part of the damage, you are liable for all damage
Case of two who dig a well: Reuven digs nine tefachim (liable for damage), Shimon adds tenth (liable for death and damage)
Debate with Rabbi: Rabbi holds the last one is chayav for death but damages split; Rabbanan hold last one chayav for all
Distinction: only when transformation occurs does last person become fully liable; proportional liability when both maintain same category
Rav Pappa: distinction between liability for death (all agree) and liability for damage (debated)

macro_segments.title: Challenges to the Two-Diggers Case: When Is Last Person Fully Liable for Partial Contribution?

micro_segments.title: Rav Zeira's challenge: other cases exist where one does little and is liable for everything
Case of five people watching ox together: one negligent; either obvious or not true upon examination
Case of adding wood to friend's fire: either obvious or not true whether last person is fully chayav
Tosafot's response: not about toothpick in bonfire; questions thresholds of contributory liability and proportional vs full

macro_segments.title: The Bench Case: Preventing Someone from Rising vs Adding Weight; Kocho Kegufoy Dami

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa's braita: five people sit on bench safely; sixth sits and it breaks—last one fully chayav
Examination: either would not break without him (obvious) or would break anyway (not true)
Solution: bench would break in two hours; sixth person causes break in one hour—acceleration of damage
Case of holding someone down on bench: kocho kegufoy dami—your force is like your body in causing liability
Principle clarified: wherever your body would break it, your koach (force/energy) also breaks it

macro_segments.title: Ten People Beating Case: Sequential vs Simultaneous Causation and the Debate with Rabbi Yehuda Ben Beseira

micro_segments.title: Braita: ten people beat victim with sticks simultaneously or sequentially—all exempt from liability
Rabbi Yehuda Ben Beseira: if sequential, last one is chayav because he brought about death (kirev misaso)
Gemara resolves: mishna is not about death penalty (not relevant to Masechet Nezikin) and follows Rabbanan, not minority view
Summary: recurring theme throughout masechet of contributory negligence, last-actor liability, and when parties are equally vs proportionally liable
